description.task: Use GROUP BY to group rows and compute aggregate statistics per group.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE orders (
  order_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
  customer   VARCHAR(50),
  category   VARCHAR(40),
  amount     DECIMAL(8,2),
  order_date DATE
);

INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1,  'Alice', 'Electronics',  999.99, '2026-01-05'),
  (2,  'Bob',   'Furniture',    250.00, '2026-01-06'),
  (3,  'Alice', 'Stationery',     5.99, '2026-01-07'),
  (4,  'Carol', 'Electronics',  299.99, '2026-01-08'),
  (5,  'Bob',   'Electronics',   19.99, '2026-01-09'),
  (6,  'Alice', 'Furniture',    175.00, '2026-01-10'),
  (7,  'Dave',  'Stationery',     8.99, '2026-01-11'),
  (8,  'Carol', 'Electronics',  999.99, '2026-01-12'),
  (9,  'Dave',  'Furniture',    350.00, '2026-01-13'),
  (10, 'Alice', 'Electronics',   59.99, '2026-01-14');

-- Group 1: Total orders and revenue per customer
SELECT
  customer,
  COUNT(*) AS order_count,
  SUM(amount) AS total_spent,
  AVG(amount) AS avg_order_value
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

-- Group 2: Revenue per category
SELECT
  category,
  COUNT(*) AS order_count,
  SUM(amount) AS category_revenue
FROM orders
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category_revenue DESC;
instructions: Customer query: Alice has 4 orders totalling ~1240.97, Carol has 2 (~1299.98), Dave 2 (~358.99), Bob 2 (~269.99).
Category query: Electronics dominates in revenue.
Add a GROUP BY on (customer, category) to see revenue per customer per category.
In a comment: why must every selected column either be in GROUP BY or wrapped in an aggregate function?
description.hint: Non-aggregated columns not in GROUP BY cause an ambiguity error — the database doesn't know which row's value to use for the group. Every column in SELECT must be either grouped or aggregated.
description.expectedOutput:
By customer: Alice highest total, ~4 orders
By category: Electronics > Furniture > Stationery

description.task: Use the HAVING clause to filter groups after aggregation — the equivalent of WHERE but for grouped results.
description.context: WHERE filters individual rows BEFORE grouping. HAVING filters whole groups AFTER aggregation. A common mistake is using WHERE with aggregate functions — that must be HAVING.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE employees (
  emp_id     INT PRIMARY KEY,
  name       VARCHAR(60),
  department VARCHAR(40),
  salary     DECIMAL(10,2)
);

INSERT INTO employees VALUES
  (1,  'Alice',  'Engineering',  95000),
  (2,  'Bob',    'Marketing',    65000),
  (3,  'Carol',  'Engineering',  88000),
  (4,  'Dave',   'HR',           55000),
  (5,  'Eve',    'Engineering', 105000),
  (6,  'Frank',  'Marketing',    70000),
  (7,  'Grace',  'HR',           58000),
  (8,  'Henry',  'Engineering',  91000),
  (9,  'Iris',   'Marketing',    68000);

-- HAVING: only departments with more than 2 employees
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS headcount, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

-- HAVING: departments where total payroll exceeds 200000
SELECT department, SUM(salary) AS total_payroll
FROM employees
GROUP BY department
HAVING SUM(salary) > 200000
ORDER BY total_payroll DESC;

-- WHERE + HAVING combined: senior employees (salary > 60000) grouped by dept
-- only departments where the avg senior salary > 80000
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS senior_count, AVG(salary) AS avg_senior_salary
FROM employees
WHERE salary > 60000
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(salary) > 80000
ORDER BY avg_senior_salary DESC;
instructions: Query 1: Engineering (4 employees) and Marketing (3) should appear; HR (2) excluded.
Query 2: Engineering total payroll = 379000 > 200K. Marketing = 203000. HR = 113000 (excluded).
Query 3: Only Engineering (senior avg ~94750) should appear.
Add a comment: what error would occur if you wrote WHERE COUNT(*) > 2 instead of HAVING?
description.hint: WHERE COUNT(*) > 2 produces 'aggregate functions are not allowed in WHERE' error. WHERE runs before grouping, so no aggregates exist yet at that stage.
description.expectedOutput:
Q1: Engineering(4 employees), Marketing(3)
Q2: Engineering(379000), Marketing(203000)
Q3: Engineering only

description.task: Use GROUP BY ROLLUP to generate subtotals and grand totals in a single query.
description.context: ROLLUP extends GROUP BY to generate all levels of subtotals automatically — useful for reports that need region totals + a grand total in one query.
description.starterCode:
CREATE TABLE regional_sales (
  region   VARCHAR(30),
  quarter  VARCHAR(5),
  product  VARCHAR(60),
  revenue  DECIMAL(10,2)
);

INSERT INTO regional_sales VALUES
  ('North', 'Q1', 'Laptop',  50000),
  ('North', 'Q1', 'Mouse',    5000),
  ('North', 'Q2', 'Laptop',  60000),
  ('North', 'Q2', 'Monitor', 30000),
  ('South', 'Q1', 'Laptop',  40000),
  ('South', 'Q1', 'Keyboard', 8000),
  ('South', 'Q2', 'Laptop',  45000),
  ('South', 'Q2', 'Mouse',    4000),
  ('West',  'Q1', 'Monitor', 20000),
  ('West',  'Q2', 'Laptop',  35000);

-- ROLLUP: revenue per region + per quarter + grand total
SELECT
  COALESCE(region,  'ALL REGIONS') AS region,
  COALESCE(quarter, 'ALL QUARTERS') AS quarter,
  SUM(revenue) AS total_revenue
FROM regional_sales
GROUP BY ROLLUP(region, quarter)
ORDER BY region NULLS LAST, quarter NULLS LAST;
instructions: Run all INSERT and the ROLLUP query.
Identify the subtotal rows: North total, South total, West total.
Identify the grand total row at the bottom (region=ALL REGIONS).
Add a comment: what is the difference between ROLLUP and CUBE in GROUP BY extensions?
description.hint: ROLLUP generates N+1 grouping levels (hierarchy). CUBE generates all possible combinations of grouping columns (2^N subsets). For (region, quarter): ROLLUP gives 3 levels, CUBE gives 4 combinations.
description.expectedOutput:
North/Q1: 55000, North/Q2: 90000, North subtotal: 145000
South/Q1: 48000, South/Q2: 49000, South subtotal: 97000
West totals + Grand total
